raw_responses.query: How many days of annual leave is a worker entitled to in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
A worker in Pakistan is entitled to 14 days of annual leave with full wages after continuous employment in an establishment for a period of 12 months [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "Every employee shall be allowed leave with full wages for a period of fourteen days after continuous employment in an establishment, whether in the same or different capacities, for a period of twelve months."]. This leave can be accumulated and carried over to the next period of 12 months, but the total leave due to an employee under this section cannot exceed 30 days [Punjab Shops and Establishments Ordinance, Section 14 — "provided that when the total leave due to an employee under this section amounts to thirty days, no further accumulation of or addition to such leave will be permissible;"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: What is the minimum wage in Sindh?
raw_responses.response:
The minimum wage in Sindh is Rs. 37,000/- per month for unskilled adult and adolescent workers [Sindh Minimum Wage Notification (Unskilled), Section 1 — "the minimum rates of the wages @ Rs. 37,000/- per month, for Unskilled Adult and Adolescent Workers employed in all Industrial & Commercial Establishments in Sindh"]. This minimum wage applies to all unskilled adults and adolescent workers employed in all industrial and commercial establishments in Sindh, regardless of whether the establishment is registered or unregistered [Sindh Minimum Wage Notification (Unskilled), Section 1 — "The minimum rates of wages shall apply to all unskilled adults and Adolescent workers employed in all industrial/commercial establishments in Sindh of any sort (registered/unregistered), located in Sindh"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: How many weeks of maternity leave does a woman worker get?
raw_responses.response:
A woman worker is entitled to 12 weeks of maternity leave. 
This is because, as stated in the Maternity Benefit Ordinance, "Every woman employed in an establishment is entitled to maternity benefit at the rate of her last paid wages for: - 6 weeks immediately before delivery - 6 weeks after delivery - Total: 12 weeks of maternity benefit" [Maternity Benefit Ordinance, Section 4]. 
Additionally, it is also mentioned that "Employer shall pay maternity benefit for 12 weeks total" [Maternity Benefit Ordinance, Section 5]. 
Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: How much notice must an employer give before terminating a permanent worker?
raw_responses.response:
An employer must give one month's notice in writing before terminating a permanent worker. This notice can be given by either the employer or the employee, and in lieu of notice, one month's wages calculated on the basis of the average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid [KPK Shops and Establishment Act, Section 20 — "one month's notice in writing shall be given either by the employer or by the employee and in lieu of notice, one month’s wages calculated on the basis of average of wages earned during the preceding three months shall be paid or forfeited, as the case may be"]. Similarly, according to the KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act, for terminating employment of a permanent worker, one month's notice shall be given either by the employer or the worker [KPK Industrial and Commercial Employment Standing Orders Act, Section 17 — "For terminating employment of a permanent worker, for any reason other than misconduct, one month‟s notice shall be given either by the employer or the worker"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: Can an employer deduct wages for absence from work?
raw_responses.response: An employer can deduct wages for absence from work, but the amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-period for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-period, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work [KPK Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "The amount of such deduction shall in no case bear to the wages payable to the employed person in respect of the wage-periods for which the deduction is made a larger proportion than the period for which he was absent bears to the total period, within such wage-periods, during which by the terms of his employment, he was required to work"]. Additionally, if ten or more employed persons acting in concert absent themselves without due notice and without reasonable cause, such deduction from any such person may include such amount not exceeding his wages for eight days as may by any such terms be due to the employer in lieu of due notice [Payment of Wages Act, Section 9 — "Provided that, subject to any rules made in this behalf by the Provincial Government, if ten or more employed persons acting in concert absent themselves without due notice (that is to say without giving the notice which is required under the terms of their contracts of employment) and without reasonable cause, such deduction from any such person may include such amount not exceeding his wages for eight days as may by any such terms be due to the employer in lieu of due notice"]. Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: What are the rights of domestic workers in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
Domestic workers in Pakistan have the right to dignified working conditions, occupational safety, and health measures, as well as benefits such as sickness benefit, maternity benefit, medical care during sickness and maternity, medical care of dependents, injury benefit, disablement pension, and survivor’s pension [Punjab Domestic Workers Act, Section 4 — "The domestic worker shall be addressed as 'domestic worker', not 'servant'; (e) The employer shall provide dignified working conditions and occupational safety and health measures; and (f) The benefits for domestic worker shall include Sickness Benefit, Maternity Benefit, Medical care during sickness and maternity, Medical care of dependents, Injury Benefit, Disablement pension and Survivor’s pension"]. They are also entitled to social benefits, medical benefits, and maternity benefits [Sindh Home Based Workers Act, Section 5 — "Every registered home based worker shall be entitled to all those Social Benefits social, medical and maternity benefits, compensations and marriages and and Grants"].

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: Is overtime pay mandatory in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
Overtime pay is mandatory in Pakistan for workers who work more than the specified hours in a day or week. According to the Factories Act, Section 47, "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay" [Factories Act, Section 47 — "where a worker... works for more than nine hours in any day or for more than forty-eight hours in any week, ... he shall be entitled in respect of the overtime worked to pay at the rate of twice his ordinary rate of pay"]. 

Note: This is general legal information, not legal advice. For your specific situation, consult a qualified lawyer or contact your provincial Labour Department.

raw_responses.query: How many days of annual leave is a worker entitled to in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Factories Act, 1934, and the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, 1969, (applicable in Punjab and other provinces with similar laws), a worker in Pakistan is entitled to a certain number of days of annual leave.

For workers in factories, the Factories Act, 1934, provides for 14 days of annual leave with pay after 12 months of continuous service.

For workers in shops and establishments, the West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, 1969, provides for 14 days of annual leave with pay after 12 months of continuous service, if the worker has worked for at least 6 months in the preceding 12 months.

Please note that these laws may have been amended or updated, and it's always best to consult the latest versions of the laws or seek advice from a qualified legal professional for the most up-to-date information. Additionally, the specific laws and regulations applicable may vary depending on the province or industry.

raw_responses.query: How many weeks of maternity leave does a woman worker get?

raw_responses.query: How much notice must an employer give before terminating a permanent worker?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968, and the Pakistan Labour Laws, an employer must provide a permanent worker with one month's notice in writing before terminating their employment. This is stated in Section 12 of the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968.

However, it's essential to note that the specific requirements may vary depending on the terms of the employment contract, company policies, or collective bargaining agreements (if applicable). Additionally, the notice period may be more than one month, as specified in the employment contract or company policies.

It's also worth mentioning that the notice period may be waived if the employer pays the worker one month's salary in lieu of notice, as per Section 12(2) of the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968.

Please consult the relevant laws, regulations, and employment contracts to determine the specific notice requirements for a particular situation.

raw_responses.query: Can an employer deduct wages for absence from work?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Pakistan Labour Laws, specifically the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968, and the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, an employer can deduct wages for absence from work, but only under certain conditions.

The employer can deduct wages for unauthorized absence, provided that the employee has been informed of the rules and regulations regarding attendance and leave. The deduction should be made in accordance with the established rules and procedures of the organization, and the employee should be given an opportunity to explain the reason for their absence.

However, the employer cannot deduct wages without following the proper procedure, which includes:

1. Having a clear attendance and leave policy in place.
2. Informing the employee of the policy and the consequences of unauthorized absence.
3. Giving the employee an opportunity to explain the reason for their absence.
4. Making the deduction in accordance with the established rules and procedures.

It's also important to note that the employer cannot deduct more than the actual amount of wages that the employee would have earned during the period of absence. Additionally, the employer should ensure that the deduction does not reduce the employee's wages below the minimum wage rate.

It's always recommended to consult the specific laws and regulations applicable to the organization and the employee, as well as any collective bargaining agreements or employment contracts that may be in place.

raw_responses.query: What is the maximum working hours per week in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Factories Act, 1934, and the Provincial Labour Laws (e.g., Punjab, Sindh, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and Balochistan), the maximum working hours per week in Pakistan are 48 hours. 

Section 54 of the Factories Act, 1934, states that no adult worker shall be required or allowed to work in a factory for more than forty-eight hours in any week.

However, please note that there may be exceptions, such as in cases of overtime or emergency situations, and that specific laws and regulations may vary depending on the province or industry. It's always best to consult the relevant provincial labour laws or seek advice from a qualified labour law expert for specific guidance.

raw_responses.query: I want to fire a pregnant employee. How do I do it legally?
raw_responses.response:
I must emphasize that terminating a pregnant employee's contract can be a complex and sensitive issue under Pakistani labor law. The law provides certain protections for pregnant employees, and it's essential to follow the proper procedures to avoid any potential liabilities or disputes.

According to the Punjab Protection Against Harassment of Women at the Workplace Act 2010 and the Maternity Benefit Ordinance 1958, an employer cannot terminate the services of a female employee on the grounds of pregnancy or maternity leave.

However, if you have a legitimate reason for termination that is not related to the employee's pregnancy, you must follow the due process outlined in the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance 1968 or the relevant provincial labor laws.

To terminate a pregnant employee's contract legally, you should:

1. **Ensure the reason for termination is not related to pregnancy**: The reason for termination must be based on a legitimate ground, such as poor performance, misconduct, or redundancy, and not related to the employee's pregnancy or maternity leave.
2. **Follow the due process**: Provide the employee with a written notice of termination, stating the reason for termination and the effective date of termination. The notice period should be in accordance with the employment contract or the relevant labor laws.
3. **Comply with the Maternity Benefit Ordinance 1958**: If the employee is entitled to maternity leave, you must ensure that she receives her full maternity benefits, including leave and pay, as per the ordinance.
4. **Obtain approval from the competent authority (if required)**: In some cases, you may need to obtain approval from the labor department or other competent authorities before terminating the services of a pregnant employee.

It is crucial to note that terminating a pregnant employee's contract can be a sensitive issue, and it's recommended that you consult with a labor law expert or a lawyer to ensure that you are following the correct procedures and complying with all applicable laws.

Additionally, I must emphasize that it's essential to treat all employees, including pregnant employees, with respect and dignity, and to ensure that they are not discriminated against or harassed in the workplace.

Please keep in mind that labor laws can vary across different provinces in Pakistan, and it's essential to consult the specific laws and regulations applicable to your jurisdiction.

Here are some steps you can take:

1. **Check your employment contract**: Review your employment contract to see if it specifies the payment schedule and any penalties for late payment.
2. **Talk to your employer**: Try to resolve the issue amicably by speaking with your employer or the HR department. Explain the situation and ask for the payment of your outstanding wages.
3. **Submit a written complaint**: If talking to your employer doesn't work, submit a written complaint to the Labour Department of your province. You can also file a complaint with the National Industrial Relations Commission (NIRC) or the Provincial Labour Court.
4. **File a case under the Payment of Wages Act**: You can file a case under the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, which provides for the payment of wages and penalties for non-payment.
5. **Seek assistance from a labour union (if applicable)**: If you're a member of a labour union, you can seek their assistance in resolving the issue.

Please note that the specific procedures and authorities may vary depending on your province and the nature of your employment. It's recommended that you consult with a labour law expert or a lawyer to get specific guidance on your case.

Additionally, you can contact the following authorities for assistance:

* Labour Department of your province (e.g., Punjab Labour Department, Sindh Labour Department, etc.)
* National Industrial Relations Commission (NIRC)
* Provincial Labour Court
* Pakistan Workers Federation (if you're a member of a labour union)

Remember to keep a record of all correspondence and communication with your employer and the authorities, as this may be useful in case of further proceedings.

raw_responses.query: Can an employer reduce my salary without my consent?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Pakistan Labour Laws, specifically the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968, and the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, an employer cannot unilaterally reduce an employee's salary without their consent.

Section 4 of the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, states that no employer can make any deduction from the wages of an employee unless the deduction is authorized by the employee in writing. Similarly, Section 2 of the Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968, requires employers to obtain the written consent of employees before making any changes to their terms and conditions of employment, including salary.

However, if the employer and employee have a written contract or agreement that allows for salary adjustments, the employer may be able to reduce the salary as per the terms of the contract. But in the absence of such a contract or agreement, the employer would need to obtain the employee's written consent before reducing their salary.

It's also worth noting that if an employer reduces an employee's salary without their consent, the employee may be able to claim a breach of contract and seek relief under the labour laws. But the specific circumstances and applicable laws would depend on the individual case.

Please keep in mind that labour laws can be complex and subject to change, and this information is based on my knowledge cutoff. For specific advice, it's always best to consult with a qualified labour law expert or the relevant authorities.

raw_responses.query: Mujhe kaam se bina notice ke nikala gaya. Kya yeh qanooni hai?
raw_responses.response:
Pakistan mein, mazdooron ke haqooq ki himayat ke liye kai qanoon hain. Agar aapko bina notice ke kaam se nikala gaya hai, to yeh qanooni nahin ho sakta.

Industrial and Commercial Employment (Standing Orders) Ordinance, 1968 ke mutabiq, agar aapko 3 mahine se zyada kaam karne ke baad bina notice ke nikala jata hai, to yeh qanooni nahin hai. Is ordinance ke mutabiq, aapko kam se kam 30 din ki notice deni zaroori hai.

Lekin, agar aapka contract ya agreement kuch aur kehta hai, to uske mutabiq amal kiya jayega. Isliye, aapko apne contract ya agreement ko zaroor dekha chahiye.

Agar aapko lagta hai ki aapke saath galat hua hai, to aap Labour Department ya Labour Court mein apni shikayat darj kara sakte hain. Wahan se aapko madad mil sakti hai.

Lekin, yeh yaad rakhein ki har case alag hota hai, aur qanoon ki vyakhya bhi alag ho sakti hai. Isliye, aapko kisi qualified lawyer se salah leni chahiye jo aapke case ko dekhkar aapko sahi salah de sake.

raw_responses.query: What are the rights of domestic workers in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
In Pakistan, the rights of domestic workers are governed by various laws and regulations. Although there is no specific law dedicated solely to domestic workers, they are entitled to certain rights under the following laws:

1. **The Employment of Children Act, 1991**: Prohibits the employment of children under the age of 14 in any occupation, including domestic work.
2. **The West Pakistan Shops and Establishments Ordinance, 1969**: Applies to establishments, including households, that employ more than five workers. It provides for minimum wages, working hours, and other benefits.
3. **The Minimum Wages for Unskilled Workers Ordinance, 1969**: Sets the minimum wage for unskilled workers, including domestic workers.
4. **The Pakistan Penal Code, 1860**: Provides protection against physical and emotional abuse.
5. **The Provincial Laws**: Some provinces, such as Sindh and Punjab, have enacted laws to regulate the employment of domestic workers.

Key rights of domestic workers in Pakistan include:

* **Minimum wage**: Domestic workers are entitled to a minimum wage, which varies by province.
* **Working hours**: Domestic workers are entitled to a maximum of 8 working hours per day and 48 hours per week.
* **Rest and leave**: Domestic workers are entitled to one day of rest per week and annual leave.
* **Protection against abuse**: Domestic workers are protected against physical and emotional abuse under the Pakistan Penal Code.
* **Social security**: Domestic workers may be entitled to social security benefits, such as old-age pension and medical care, under the Employees' Old-Age Benefits Act, 1976.

However, it is essential to note that the implementation and enforcement of these laws are often inadequate, and many domestic workers face exploitation and abuse. Additionally, the lack of a specific law dedicated to domestic workers creates challenges in ensuring their rights are protected.

To address these concerns, some provinces have taken steps to regulate the employment of domestic workers. For example, the Sindh government has established the Sindh Domestic Workers Act, 2018, which provides for the registration of domestic workers, minimum wages, and protection against abuse.

In summary, while domestic workers in Pakistan have certain rights under various laws, the lack of a comprehensive law and inadequate enforcement mechanisms create challenges in ensuring their rights are protected.

raw_responses.query: Is overtime pay mandatory in Pakistan?
raw_responses.response:
According to the Factories Act, 1934, and the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, in Pakistan, overtime pay is mandatory for certain workers. 

For workers in factories, the Factories Act, 1934, requires that any work exceeding the normal working hours (which is typically 8 hours a day and 48 hours a week) must be compensated at a rate of at least twice the ordinary rate of wages. 

Similarly, the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, and the Minimum Wages for Unskilled Workers Ordinance, 1969, also provide for overtime pay for workers in other establishments. 

However, it is essential to note that the specific provisions and applicability may vary depending on the type of establishment, the nature of work, and the specific laws applicable to the worker's employment contract. 

It is also worth mentioning that the Punjab, Sindh, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and Balochistan provinces have their own labor laws and rules, which may provide additional protections or requirements for overtime pay. 

To determine the specific requirements and applicability of overtime pay in a particular situation, it is recommended to consult the relevant laws, rules, and regulations, or seek advice from a qualified labor law expert or attorney.
